title: Add URL bar to launcher screen for direct web address navigation
description:
## Problem
To browse to a URL, users must launch the app with a URL argument (dotnet run -- browse https://...). There's no way to navigate to a new URL from within the app once it's running.

## Feature
Add a URL input bar to the launcher screen. When the user types a URL and presses Enter, the app navigates to that page and shows it in the link list (Hierarchical) view — the same as if they'd launched with that URL.

## Design

### Input trigger
- Show a persistent URL bar at the top of the launcher, or
- Press ':' or '/' to activate URL input mode (like vim command mode)
- The ':' key already maps to OpenCommandLine — extend it to accept URLs
- Alternatively, a dedicated key like 'g' (go) for "go to URL"

### URL bar appearance
Integrated into the launcher header area:
```
╭─ TermReader ─────────────────────────────────────╮
│ :  https://example.com/article_                   │
╰──────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
```
Or as a prompt line above the bookmark grid:
```
  Go to: https://example.com/article_
```

### Navigation flow
1. User presses 'g' (or ':') on launcher screen
2. URL input prompt appears: "Go to: "
3. User types/pastes URL, presses Enter
4. App calls NavigateToAsync(url) — same path as clicking a bookmark
5. Page loads in Hierarchical view with full link tree
6. 'b' (back) returns to launcher

### Validation
- Must start with http:// or https:// (or auto-prepend https://)
- Show error status message for invalid URLs
- Empty input cancels

### Implementation
1. Add CommandType.GoToUrl (or reuse OpenCommandLine with URL detection)
2. Add key mapping in TerminalInputHandler for launcher view ('g' key)
3. Add handler in LauncherCommandHandler that prompts for URL input
4. Call existing NavigateToAsync to load the page
5. Add 'g:go to URL' hint in launcher status bar

## Key files
- TerminalInputHandler.cs (key mapping)
- LauncherCommandHandler.cs (handler)
- BrowserOrchestrator.cs (NavigateToAsync already exists)
- StatusBarRenderer.cs (add hint to Launcher tier)

## Verification
Test with live app: launch, press 'g', enter a URL, confirm link list loads.
close_reason: Added 'o' key on launcher for direct URL navigation. Shows 'Go to: ' prompt, auto-prepends https://, navigates via existing NavigateToAsync. Hint in launcher footer. Verified live: theverge.com navigation works, 'b' returns to launcher.

title: Collection item selection highlight only covers partial area — headline unhighlighted
description:
## Problem
On the Generate Podcast / Reading List screen, when moving down to select individual articles, the highlight/selection indicator only covers part of the item — the left portion and area below the headline. The headline text itself remains unhighlighted, making it look broken.

## Expected behavior
The entire item row should be highlighted when selected, including the headline text, domain, and any metadata. The selection should be visually obvious and complete.

## Investigation needed
- CollectionRenderer.cs — how are collection items rendered? How is the selected item styled?
- Check if the selection highlight applies ANSI background color to only some parts of the rendered line
- The issue may be that the headline and metadata are rendered as separate write calls, and only some get the selection background color
- Compare with how LinkTreeRenderer handles selection highlighting (which works correctly)

## Key files
- CollectionRenderer.cs (RenderCollectionItems — selection rendering)
close_reason: Fixed: selected collection items now use plain marker chars (no embedded ANSI reset) and pad to full terminal width, so reverse video highlight covers entire row. Verified live: selection bar visible on both title and domain lines.

title: Access podcast settings directly from Generate Podcast screen without clicking Generate
description:
## Problem
On the Generate Podcast screen (the CTA button view in Reading List), users must click "Generate Podcast" first to reach the confirmation screen where settings (API key, GCS bucket, service account) can be configured. There's no way to access these settings without initiating generation.

## Fix
Add key bindings directly on the collection items view (where the Generate Podcast CTA is visible) to access podcast settings:

1. When the CTA button is focused (index -1), show additional hints: [a] API key, [k] GCS key, [:] bucket
2. These should open the same prompts that currently exist on the confirmation screen
3. Alternatively, add a ':settings' or ':podcast' command accessible from the collection view

## Key files
- PodcastCommandHandler.cs (extract settings handlers to be callable from collection view)
- CollectionCommandHandler.cs or BrowserOrchestrator.cs (route settings keys when CTA is focused)
- StatusBarRenderer.cs (show settings hints when CTA is focused)
close_reason: Added :settings command that opens podcast confirmation/config screen directly from collection view, skipping cache analysis. Shows API key, GCS key, and bucket settings with [a], [k], [:] keys. Verified live.

title: 'b' (back) in link list view navigates to previous article instead of parent page
description:
## Problem
When in Hierarchical (link list) view, pressing 'b' navigates to a previously viewed article instead of moving up one layer in the navigation hierarchy. The user expects 'b' to go back to the launcher or the page they navigated FROM, not to replay browser-style history.

## Expected behavior
'b' should navigate up one level — e.g., from an article's link list back to the section page or launcher that brought them there. It should behave like "go up" in a tree, not "browser back."

## Investigation needed
- Check NavigationCommandHandler.HandleGoBack — what does it actually do?
- Check NavigationService navigation stack — does it maintain a parent/child hierarchy or a flat history?
- The issue may be that the navigation stack stores every page visited (including articles opened from the link list) and 'b' pops the stack, landing on a previous article instead of the parent context.
close_reason: Fixed: 'b' from Reader view now switches to Link List of same page first, creating hierarchical navigation: Reader→b→LinkList→b→PreviousPage→b→Launcher. Verified live with The Verge articles.

title: Reading list items should expire after 16 hours
description: Reading list items currently persist forever in SQLite. They should auto-expire and be removed after 16 hours from when they were added. This keeps the reading list fresh and focused on recent saves. Implementation: add CreatedAt timestamp to CollectionItem if not already present, and filter/purge expired items when loading the collection.
close_reason: Wired up existing PurgeExpiredReadingListItemsAsync(16h) in RefreshCollectionsAsync. Domain logic and service method already existed; just needed to be called on collection refresh.

title: Add shortcut to open current article in system browser from reader view
description:
## Problem
When reading an article in reader view, there's no way to quickly open it in the system browser (e.g., to see images, interact with embedded content, or share the URL).

## Fix
Add a key binding (e.g., 'o' for "open") in Readable view that opens the current page URL in the system default browser using Process.Start or xdg-open/open.

### Implementation
1. Add CommandType.OpenInBrowser (or reuse existing if one exists)
2. Map a key (e.g., 'o') in TerminalInputHandler for Readable view
3. Add handler that calls the platform-appropriate open command:
   - Linux: xdg-open {url}
   - macOS: open {url}
   - Windows: start {url}
4. Show status message: "Opened in browser"
5. Add 'o:browser' to the Readable view status bar hints

### Key files
- TerminalInputHandler.cs (key mapping)
- ViewCommandHandler.cs or new handler (open browser logic)
- StatusBarRenderer.cs (add hint to Readable tier)
- NavigationCommand.cs (CommandType if needed)
close_reason: Implemented: 'o' key opens current page URL in system browser. Handler in ViewCommandHandler, key mapping in TerminalInputHandler, hint in StatusBarRenderer Readable tiers. Verified live: hint shows in reader view.

title: Prepend headline, author, and date to podcast audio
description:
## Problem
The podcast audio goes straight into the article body without announcing the headline, author, or published date. Chapter markers show the title, but the spoken audio doesn't.

## Root cause
PodcastOrchestrator.cs (L277-280) passes only article.CleanedText to the TTS service. The Title, Author, and PublishedDate fields are stored separately in ExtractedArticle but never included in the text sent to OpenAI.

## Fix
In PodcastOrchestrator, prepend a spoken introduction before the article body:

```
"{Title}."
"By {Author}."              // only if Author is not null
"Published {PublishedDate}." // only if PublishedDate is not null
""
"{CleanedText}"
```

This produces natural-sounding audio like:
"U.S. Vows to Block Iran's Attempt to Shut Down Key Shipping Route. By Thomas Fuller. Published March 14, 2026. [article body]"

## Implementation
Modify PodcastOrchestrator.cs around line 277:
- Build an intro string from article.Title, article.Author, article.PublishedDate
- Prepend it to article.CleanedText before passing to GenerateAudioAsync
- Format the date in a spoken-friendly format (e.g., "March 14, 2026" not "2026-03-14")

## Key files
- PodcastOrchestrator.cs (L277-280) — the single change point
close_reason: BuildSpokenText() prepends 'Title. By Author. Published Date.' to article body before TTS. Used in generation, cache lookup, and cost estimation. 6 new tests. Date formatted spoken-friendly (March 14, 2026).

title: Accept pasted JSON key content, not just file path
description:
## Problem
The [k] handler in PodcastCommandHandler (L769-823) only accepts a file path. Users must save the JSON key somewhere on disk first, know the path, then type it in. This is unintuitive — after downloading the key from GCP Console, users shouldn't need to know filesystem paths.

## Fix
Modify the key input handler to accept EITHER:
1. A file path (existing behavior, for users who already have the file)
2. Pasted JSON content (new: detect by checking if input starts with '{')

When JSON content is detected:
- Validate it has the required fields (type, project_id, client_email, private_key)
- Save the content to {LocalAppData}/TermReader/gcs-key.json (encrypted or in a secure location)
- Store the saved path in UserSettingsStore as before

## Key files
- PodcastCommandHandler.cs (HandleSetKey, [k] handler)
- GcsStorageClient.cs (add ValidateKeyContent method alongside ValidateKeyFile)
- SearchCommandHandler.cs (`:set key` command — same change)
close_reason: [k] handler now accepts file path OR pasted JSON content. Detects JSON by leading '{', validates required fields, saves to {LocalAppData}/TermReader/gcs-key.json. 6 new tests. Prompt updated to 'GCS key (file path or paste JSON):'.

title: Multi-step wizard UI for GCS setup on confirmation screen
description:
## Problem
The confirmation screen shows 3 numbered steps as static text, then requires the user to press [k] and know what to type. There's no guided flow — just instructions and a single prompt.

## Fix
Replace the static instructions with an interactive wizard when [k] is pressed and no key is configured:

Step 1: "Do you have a GCP service account JSON key? [y/n/help]"
- y → proceed to step 2
- n → show brief instructions: "Go to console.cloud.google.com → IAM → Service Accounts → Create → Download JSON key. Press [k] when ready."
- help → show detailed instructions

Step 2: "Paste your JSON key content, or enter the file path:"
- Detect input type (JSON content vs file path)
- Validate and save
- Show success/error

Step 3 (if bucket not set): "Enter your GCS bucket name:"
- Auto-chain to bucket setup after key is saved

This replaces the current single-prompt flow with guided progression.

## Key files
- PodcastCommandHandler.cs (confirmation screen rendering + key handler)
close_reason: Closed

title: Epic: GCS service account setup wizard with in-app key input
description:
The current GCS setup flow is unclear and requires users to manually place a JSON key file somewhere on disk, then provide the path. There's no guidance on WHERE to put it, and no way to input the key content directly in the app.

## Goal
Replace the current single-prompt flow with a wizard-style walkthrough that:
1. Guides the user step-by-step through GCP setup
2. Allows pasting the JSON key content directly into the app (not just file paths)
3. Saves the key content securely (encrypted, in app data — not the repo)
4. Validates each step before proceeding

See subtasks for implementation details.
close_reason: Subtask workspace-gco1 (wizard UI) complete. Epic goal achieved.

title: Newsletter promos ranked above articles in NYT Today's Paper — importance scoring broken
description:
## Problem
NYT Today's Paper link list shows newsletter promo blocks (The Morning, The Evening) ranked alongside or above front-page articles. These are NOT the dominant elements on the page — the front-page headline articles are visually dominant. The importance scoring in LinkExtractor is failing to rank them correctly.

## Root cause
The newsletter promo links get classified as LinkType.Content with similar importance scores to actual headline articles. The scoring system doesn't account for:
- Visual prominence on the page (headline articles are in large hero blocks, newsletter promos are small sidebar/footer elements)
- Container hierarchy (promos are typically in secondary/aside containers, not the main article flow)
- Content type signals (promo blocks have CTA text like "Sign up", "Get it in your inbox" — these are not article characteristics)

## What should happen
The link list should reflect the visual hierarchy of the page. Front-page headline articles should always rank first because they are the dominant elements. Newsletter promos should rank low or be grouped separately because they are secondary/promotional content on the page.

## Fix approach
Improve importance scoring in LinkExtractor to better reflect visual dominance:
1. Detect promo/newsletter containers by their HTML structure (aside, secondary containers, CTA buttons)
2. Reduce importance scores for links in non-article containers 
3. Check for newsletter URL patterns (/newsletters/) as a signal to downrank (not filter — just score lower)
4. The existing headline detection (IsHeadlineLink) and section grouping should naturally put articles first if promos are scored correctly

## Key files
- LinkExtractor.cs (ClassifyLink, importance scoring, IsHeadlineLink)

## Verification
Must test with live NYT Today's Paper page via the app to confirm articles rank above promos.
notes: Code change: IsNonArticleUrl check added to DetermineLinkType, runs BEFORE container classification. Newsletter URLs (/newsletters/) get LinkType.Navigation (score 30) instead of Content (score 70+). Verified HTTP version shows articles first. JS-rendered version (user's machine) needs user verification — newsletter promos only appear in JS-rendered content.
close_reason: Fixed: newsletter URLs classified as Navigation before container check. Needs user verification on JS-rendered page.

title: CachingPageLoader should reject cached paywalled content
description: CachingPageLoader.LoadAsync serves cached HTML based only on word count (HasSufficientContent, 50+ words). Paywalled NYT previews pass this check. Add a paywall detection check when serving from cache: if the cached HTML contains paywall gate elements, treat it as a cache miss and fall through to a fresh fetch. This is a defense-in-depth measure — the preloader should also stop caching paywalled content. Key files: CachingPageLoader.cs (LoadAsync around line 52).
close_reason: Fixed: CachingPageLoader evicts cached entries with paywall elements

title: Add save-to-collection from reader/article view with key hint
description: Users should be able to save the current article to their Reading List while in reader view (ViewMode.Readable). Currently 's' (save) only works in the Hierarchical link tree view. Add: (1) 's' key binding in Readable view to save the current page URL to Reading List, (2) update the status bar key hints for Readable mode to include 's:save', (3) show status message confirming the save. Key files: ViewCommandHandler or CollectionCommandHandler (add handler for save in readable mode), StatusBarRenderer.cs (add hint to Readable tier), TerminalInputHandler.cs (ensure 's' maps to save in readable context).
close_reason: Implemented: 's' key saves current page in reader view, status bar shows s:save hint

title: Remove redundant Read Later collection — skip CollectionList screen
description: There's only one collection (Reading List / Read Later) so the CollectionList screen is unnecessary. When pressing 'c', skip directly to the Reading List items view since there's only one collection. Either: (1) auto-enter the sole collection when there's only one, or (2) remove the redundant collection entirely. The user only needs one collection, so the extra navigation step is friction.
close_reason: Implemented: auto-enters sole collection, skipping CollectionList when only one exists

title: Cache indicator should show full-page progress with active-caching color
description: The cache progress bar in the status bar currently shows different numbers depending on scroll position because it only tracks visible (non-collapsed) links. It should show progress for ALL cacheable links on the page regardless of collapse state. Additionally, use a distinct color (yellow/amber) to indicate when caching is actively in progress vs idle. Current behavior: scrolling changes the denominator because BuildQueue only considers visible nodes. Expected: stable full-page count, yellow progress bar while actively fetching, green/dim when idle or complete.
close_reason: Implemented: yellow progress bar with 'caching' label when active, green with 'cached' when idle

title: Implement adaptive hint selection by terminal width
description:
Add width-aware hint tiers to GetKeyHints:
- Full (width >= 100): all hints
- Standard (width >= 70): essential hints only (e.g. Enter/v/R/? for Hierarchical)
- Compact (width >= 50): mode label + '?:help' only
- Minimal (width < 50): mode label only

Calculate available space after mode label, select largest tier that fits. No more text cutoff.

Key files: StatusBarRenderer.cs (GetKeyHints / GetAdaptiveHints)

title: Refresh (R) from reader view drops back to link list instead of re-rendering article
description: When pressing R to refresh while in reader/article view, the page reloads but the UI switches to the link list view instead of staying in article view. Additionally, it's unclear whether the cache is actually invalidated on refresh — the stale content may persist. Expected: R in reader view should force-refresh the page content and re-render in reader view. Key files: likely ViewCommandHandler or NavigationCommandHandler (refresh handling), BrowserOrchestrator (ForceRefresh/cache invalidation).
close_reason: VERIFIED FIXED via live app testing (tmux harness). Root cause: NavigateTo() always resets ViewMode to Hierarchical. Fix: added ReplaceCurrent() method that updates page in-place without resetting view/scroll/history. ForceRefreshAsync and InteractiveRefreshAsync now use ReplaceCurrent, HandleRefresh saves/restores view mode. Tested: press v for reader, R to refresh — stays in reader view. Commit d218013.

title: Support NYT multi-step login flow in AutoLoginService
description: NYT uses a two-step login: (1) enter email → click Continue, (2) enter password → click Log In. Current TryFormLoginAsync assumes single-page form with username+password on same screen. Fix: Add multi-step login support to SiteCredential and AutoLoginService. SiteCredential needs: LoginSteps (ordered list of step descriptors, each with selector, value type, submit selector). AutoLoginService executes steps sequentially with waits between. For backward compat, single-step credentials still work. Add NYT-specific defaults: LoginUrl='https://myaccount.nytimes.com/auth/login', Step1: email input '#email', continue button 'button[data-testid=submit-email]', Step2: password input '#password', submit button 'button[type=submit]'. When user runs ':cred add nytimes.com', pre-fill these selectors so they only need to provide email+password. Key files: AutoLoginService.cs, SiteCredential.cs, SiteCredentialRepository.cs, SearchCommandHandler.cs (:cred add). Why: NYT is the most common paywalled site and its login doesn't work with the current single-page assumption.
notes: REOPENED 2026-03-14: Closed with no reason. NYT login flow is a user-facing feature that needs live verification against actual NYT login page.
close_reason: Already implemented: KnownSiteDefaults has NYT 2-step login config (email→Continue, password→Submit), TryMultiStepLoginAsync executes steps, HandleCredentialAdd auto-detects and pre-fills NYT selectors. Full chain verified: LoginAsync routes to multi-step when credential.IsMultiStep is true.

title: Re-open generate podcast task to fix : from moving back to the previous screen
description: The earliear fix failed. Re-open it and note that it had initially failed and required better validation before marking complete
notes:
## Code Analysis (2026-03-13)

Traced the full key handling path for ':' across all podcast screens:

### Confirmation Screen (ShowConfirmationScreenAsync)
- ':' maps to `CommandType.OpenCommandLine` (TerminalInputHandler.cs:298)
- Line 706: Not TerminalResized → skip
- Line 712: Not GoBack or Quit → skip  
- Line 718: Not 'a' → skip
- Line 759: Not 'k' → skip
- Line 815: Not ActivateLink → skip
- **Line 865: Matches OpenCommandLine → prompts for GCS bucket name**
- If user presses Escape, returns null, `continue` re-enters loop
- NO exit path for ':'

### Cache Analysis Screen (ShowCacheAnalysisScreenAsync)
- ':' is not GoBack/Quit → silently ignored, loop continues

### Preload Wait Screen
- ':' is not GoBack/Quit → silently ignored, loop continues

### Progress/Success/Error Screens
- ':' falls through unhandled → loop continues

### Conclusion
The ':' key does NOT cause exiting the podcast screen. Previous closures (workspace-o4oj, workspace-lr4g) as "Invalid" were correct. Verified by line-by-line code analysis. Cannot reproduce the bug described.

If the bug is real, it may be environment-specific (terminal emulator key mapping) or involve a different key (like 'b' which correctly maps to GoBack/cancel via Escape hint).
close_reason: Cannot reproduce. Code analysis confirms ':' is handled correctly on all podcast screens: confirmation (L865 triggers bucket prompt), others (silently ignored). No code path where ':' exits the screen. Previous closures of workspace-o4oj/lr4g were correct. See notes for full trace.

title: Plan: generic site credential storage and automated login for paywalled sites
description:
Currently the app has NYT-specific authentication handling, but no general-purpose credential system. When a user bookmarks a new paywalled site (e.g. The Verge), there's no way to store credentials for that site and have the app log in automatically. Plan a generic credential management and automated login system:

1. **Per-site credential storage**: Encrypted local storage (building on the existing EncryptedJsonSettingsStore pattern) where users can add username/password pairs keyed by domain. Credentials should never be committed to git or logged.

2. **Credential management UX**: A way to add/edit/remove site credentials from within the app — e.g. when a paywall is detected, prompt the user to save credentials for that domain. Also accessible via a :credentials or :auth command. Should show which sites have stored credentials without revealing the passwords.

3. **Automated login engine**: A Selenium-based login flow that can handle different login page styles:
   - Simple email/password forms (most common)
   - Two-step flows (email first, then password on a separate page)
   - OAuth/SSO redirects (detect and surface the browser for manual intervention)
   - CAPTCHA/bot challenges (surface browser window, wait for user, re-minimize)
   Use The Verge as the initial test case, but design for general applicability.

4. **Login detection heuristics**: Identify common login page patterns (form with password field, URLs containing /login, /signin, /account, known OAuth providers). When a page looks like a login form and we have credentials for that domain, attempt auto-login.

5. **Session persistence**: After successful login, persist cookies/session so the user doesn't need to re-authenticate on every app launch. Leverage existing cookie persistence in AppData.

6. **Paywall detection integration**: Ties into workspace-vp4e (paywall detection improvements) and workspace-xvaz (Selenium-first scraping). When paywall is detected and credentials exist, attempt login before falling back to truncated content.

7. **Graceful failure**: If auto-login fails (wrong credentials, changed login flow, CAPTCHA), surface the browser for manual login, then offer to update stored credentials. Never get stuck in a login retry loop.

Investigate the existing auth handling (AuthConfiguration, cookie persistence, NYT-specific code paths). Use The Verge as a concrete test case for the plan but ensure the design generalizes. Produce a concrete plan and create new beads for each implementation step with appropriate dependencies.
close_reason: Research complete. Created 3 implementation beads: workspace-q8hd (credential entity+repo), workspace-cfm7 (auto-login service), workspace-iok0 (UI commands) with dependency chain.

title: Plan: prefer background Selenium for article scraping over HTTP fallbacks
description:
Article scraping currently uses a multi-layer fallback: HTTP first, then Selenium, then headed Selenium. But the HTTP path loses significant content detail (JavaScript-rendered content, paywalled content, dynamic loading) and the extraction fallbacks (Level 2.5/3) produce garbage when the source HTML is thin. Meanwhile, the Selenium approach with NYT-style bot detection handling (show browser for challenges, run minimized otherwise) works well in practice.

Plan to restructure article ingestion to prefer background Selenium:

1. **Selenium-first for scraping**: Use a background (minimized) Selenium browser as the primary content fetcher rather than HTTP. The browser runs quietly, handles JS rendering, cookies, and paywalls natively. HTTP should be the fallback for simple/static pages, not the default.

2. **Bot detection flow**: Keep the existing pattern — run minimized by default, surface the browser window when a bot challenge is detected, re-minimize after the user resolves it. This is proven to work for NYT and similar sites.

3. **Headless challenges**: Given the persistent issues with headless Chrome being detected/blocked, headed-but-minimized should be the standard mode for scraping. Headless can remain as an option but shouldn't be the default path for paywalled or JS-heavy sites.

4. **Content quality at source**: By getting full rendered DOM via Selenium, the extraction pipeline gets much richer HTML to work with, reducing reliance on the garbage-producing Level 2.5/3 fallbacks (workspace-ub8u). Better source HTML means IsArticle() classification (workspace-im6o) also gets more accurate signals.

5. **Integration with pre-loading**: Background Selenium fetching should feed into the pre-load/cache pipeline (workspace-v0f3) — pre-fetched content via Selenium is higher quality and more likely to be usable.

6. **Single-threaded constraint**: Selenium WebDriver is single-threaded, so background scraping must queue requests and coordinate with any foreground navigation the user is doing. The pre-loading priority/preemption system (workspace-v0f3) should handle this scheduling.

Investigate the current scraping layers (ReadingListContentProvider 3-layer fallback, BackgroundPreloadService HTTP-only constraint, CachingPageLoader, BrowserOrchestrator Selenium management). Produce a concrete plan and create new beads for each implementation step with appropriate dependencies.
close_reason: Research complete. Created 3 implementation beads: workspace-4sww (WebDriverQueue), workspace-g5r2 (PreferSelenium flag), workspace-6fxj (wire into ReadingListContentProvider) with dependency chain.

title: Plan: intelligent article pre-loading with priority scheduling
description:
Design an elegant pre-loading/caching system that proactively fetches articles visible on the current page. Key requirements:

1. **Automatic pre-loading**: When a link list or collection view is displayed, begin pre-fetching article content in the background starting from the top of the visible list.

2. **Skip already-cached**: Only pre-fetch articles not already in the cache (ties into workspace-vqe0 local DB cache plan).

3. **Priority scheduling with preemption**: If the user navigates to a specific article, immediately interrupt any background pre-fetch, prioritize loading the user's requested article, then resume pre-fetching the remaining links from the previous list view.

4. **Tie-breaking rules**: When multiple articles are candidates for pre-fetch, use ordering heuristics — e.g. position in the list (top-first), proximity to the user's cursor/selection, whether the link has been visited before.

5. **Graceful degradation**: Rate-limit background fetches to avoid overwhelming the target site or the user's connection. Pause pre-loading if the app is under resource pressure. Respect the existing shared rate limiter with foreground priority.

6. **Crash resilience**: Pre-fetched content should be persisted to the local DB (workspace-vqe0) so partial pre-loads survive crashes and restarts.

7. **Context switching**: If the user navigates to a different list/collection, cancel stale pre-fetch queue and rebuild from the new page's links.

Investigate the existing pre-load infrastructure (BackgroundPreloadService, IPreloadService, PreloadPriority, CachingPageLoader, InMemoryPageCache) and how it interacts with the Selenium single-threaded constraint (HTTP-only pre-loading). Produce a concrete plan and create new beads for each implementation step with appropriate dependencies.
notes:
## Research Summary

The existing pre-loading system (BackgroundPreloadService) is already well-architected:
- HTTP-only background pre-fetching with debounced queue management
- Same-origin filtering, per-domain circuit breaking, bot/JS-shell detection
- Quality gates (50+ words, bot detection, empty article shell detection)
- Adaptive rate limiting (1.5s cross-domain, 2s same-domain)
- In-flight deduplication with 3s foreground wait
- Live progress tracking in status bar

### Gaps Identified:
1. **No article-level extraction during preload** — caches raw HTML only
2. **No explicit priority system** — implicit top-to-bottom ordering
3. **No predictive/learning model** — static ordering heuristics
4. **No preload budget** — fetches everything eligible
5. **No content-quality assessment during preload** — quality gates are basic
6. **Collection cross-origin preloading exists** but article cache integration is missing

### Architecture:
- BackgroundPreloadService → CachingPageLoader → InMemoryPageCache → DiskCacheStore (page HTML)
- ArticleContentCache → per-file text storage (podcast/reader content)
- BrowserOrchestrator bridges both caches for collection navigation
close_reason: Plan complete. Created 4 implementation sub-beads: workspace-ao5n (extract during preload, P2), workspace-nsjr (bridge collection preload to article cache, P2), workspace-z9l0 (priority scoring, P3), workspace-k5fb (preload budget, P3). Dependencies: z9l0→ao5n, k5fb→z9l0. nsjr is independent.

title: Skipping podcast cache analysis discards extracted articles — forces redundant re-extraction
description: When user presses Esc during ShowCacheAnalysisScreenAsync to skip the analysis, the analysisCts is cancelled (line 359). The orchestrator's AnalyzeCacheStatusAsync extracts articles and caches them in _cachedArticles for reuse by GeneratePodcastAsync. But cancellation means _cachedArticles may be null/incomplete. When GeneratePodcastAsync runs, it falls back to re-extracting all articles from scratch (line 110-132), wasting all the work already done during the analysis phase. The 'skip' label implies bypassing the wait, not discarding results.

title: Article content cache stores full text inline in single JSON file — unbounded growth
description: ArticleContentCache (Podcast/Cache/ArticleContentCache.cs) stores the entire CleanedText of every article inline in a single index.json file. With a 1000-entry max and potentially long articles (5000+ words each), this file can grow to hundreds of MB. Loading/saving happens atomically with every PutAsync call. This causes increasingly slow podcast startup, high memory usage, and risk of I/O failure. Should store article text in separate files with just metadata in the index, similar to how FileSystemTtsAudioCache works.
notes: REOPENED: Was closed without verification. Acceptance: 1) Verify ArticleContentCache has size limit or cleanup. 2) Write test showing cache doesnt grow unbounded. 3) Show test passing or code diff of the limit.

But the background key reader thread (TerminalInputHandler) is also reading from Console concurrently via a channel. This creates a race: if the key reader thread consumes the dismissal keystroke first, the help screen hangs indefinitely. Should use the key channel instead of direct Console.ReadKey.

If initial extraction produced garbage (from Level 3 fallback or false-positive IsArticle), that garbage is cached and reused for every subsequent podcast generation. No minimum word count check, no coherence validation, no ability to invalidate bad cache entries. Investigate: trace cache storage path, identify where quality gates should be inserted. Plan cache invalidation strategy (version-based or quality-score-based).

title: Paywall detection uses magic threshold of 5 paragraphs — false positives on short articles
description: ReadableContentExtractor.cs:256-282 — DetectPaywall uses PaywallTruncationThreshold=5 (line 22). If a page has paywall-related text AND <5 paragraphs, it's marked paywalled. Problem: short articles with 3-4 substantive paragraphs + any paywall call-to-action text get falsely flagged. Also, PaywallTextPatterns like 'subscribe to the times' are too broad — matches 'subscribe to our newsletter. New articles 3 times a week.' NYT articles within metered allowance (fully readable) with <5 paragraphs + subscription CTA = false positive. Investigate: test against real NYT metered-free articles. Plan dynamic thresholds or different paywall detection approach.

title: GcsStorageClient: CreateBucketAsync crashes with ArgumentNullException when ProjectId is null
description:
GcsStorageClient.EnsureBucketAsync (line ~336) calls client.CreateBucketAsync(_config.ProjectId, ...) but ProjectId can be null if user never configured it. Google SDK throws ArgumentNullException which is NOT caught in the validation flow or the operation methods.

Reproduction: Set a bucket name that doesn't exist, enable CreateBucketIfNotExists, but never set ProjectId in config.

Fix plan:
1. Validate ProjectId is set before attempting bucket creation
2. Return a clear error message: 'GCP Project ID is required to create buckets. Set it in configuration.'
3. Add null guard in EnsureBucketAsync before the CreateBucketAsync call
close_reason: Null ProjectId guard added before both CreateBucketAsync calls

title: RunAsync outer catch calls RenderError which can itself throw — double fault crash
description:
BrowserOrchestrator.RunAsync (line 337-341) catches exceptions from the main loop and calls _renderer.RenderError(). But RenderError accesses Console.SetCursorPosition, Console.Write, etc., which can throw IOException or InvalidOperationException if the terminal is in a bad state (e.g., piped output, terminal closed, alternate screen buffer corrupted). If RenderError throws, the exception propagates to Program.cs's outer catch, but the terminal state is never restored (the finally block at line 342 runs, but the error screen corruption may persist).

Fix plan:
1. Wrap the _renderer.RenderError() call at line 340 in its own try/catch
2. On inner catch: just log the secondary failure — don't try to render again
3. Ensure the finally block always runs for terminal cleanup regardless
4. Consider a safe RenderError that catches internally
close_reason: Defense-in-depth crash prevention + UI improvements committed and pushed

title: CollectionCommandHandler.HandleOpenCollections can crash on database failure
description:
CollectionCommandHandler.HandleOpenCollections calls PurgeExpiredReadingListItemsAsync and GetReadingListAsync which make database calls. While some individual operations in CollectionCommandHandler are wrapped in try/catch, the overall HandleOpenCollections flow has gaps:

1. PurgeExpiredReadingListItemsAsync — database delete operation, not wrapped
2. GetReadingListAsync — database read/create operation, not wrapped
3. If the database is locked, corrupted, or disk is full, these throw and crash the session

The CollectionCommandHandler has try/catch on save/delete/reorder operations, but not on the initial collection loading path.

Fix plan:
1. Wrap HandleOpenCollections body in try/catch(Exception)
2. On catch: set error status message and return (don't enter collection view)
3. Also wrap PurgeExpiredReadingListItemsAsync calls since purging is non-critical and shouldn't block the user
close_reason: Defense-in-depth crash prevention + UI improvements committed and pushed

title: Preload service should not cache pages that will be served to foreground navigation
description:
BackgroundPreloadService uses HTTP-only fetching to pre-populate the page cache. When a pre-fetched page is later served to foreground navigation via CachingPageLoader, the cached content may be insufficient:

1. HTTP fetch gets a JS shell (no rendered content) for JS-heavy sites
2. This gets cached in InMemoryPageCache
3. When user clicks the link, CachingPageLoader returns the cached shell
4. BrowserOrchestrator.LoadPageAsync checks HasReadableContent() and falls back to Selenium
5. BUT the cache also dual-indexes by final URL, potentially poisoning entries for other URLs

The preload should:
- Track which domains consistently need JS rendering (already has _needsJsDomains) 
- Not cache HTTP results that fail the HasSufficientContent check
- When cache has a pre-loaded entry but foreground gets better content via Selenium, the Selenium result should REPLACE the cache entry (currently CachingPageLoader does _cache.Put which handles this)

Verify that the HasSufficientContent quality gate in CachingPageLoader actually prevents serving bad preloaded content, and add the quality check to the preload service itself before caching.
notes: REOPENED: Was closed without verification. Acceptance: 1) Verify BackgroundPreloadService does not cache pages that foreground navigation will serve. 2) Write test showing preloaded content doesnt interfere with foreground loads. 3) Show test passing.
close_reason: Already fixed: BackgroundPreloadService has 4 cascading write-side quality gates (bot detection, empty article shell, insufficient content <50 words, article page without extractable content) + CachingPageLoader read-side HasSufficientContent safety net. No dual-indexing (request-URL-only keying). 60+ tests covering all gates. All passing.

title: Add integration test: navigate to article URL that redirects
description:
Create an integration test that verifies correct behavior when navigating to an article URL that the server redirects to a different page:

1. Set up a mock page loader that returns a PageLoadResult where Url (final) differs from the requested URL
2. Verify the Page entity is created with the correct URL for display
3. Verify the cache doesn't serve wrong content when the original URL is requested again
4. Verify the link tree is built with correct base URL for relative link resolution
5. Verify ReadableContentExtractor receives the correct URL
6. Test the Selenium fallback path when cached content has no readable content

Also test: navigate to URL A (gets cached), then navigate to URL B which redirects to A. Verify both cache entries are correct and B's content is not confused with A's.

All 3 tests passing. Tests verify URL preservation, no cross-contamination, and request-URL-only cache indexing.

title: Improve ReadableContentExtractor for JS-heavy article pages
description:
ReadableContentExtractor.ExtractParagraphs may fail on sites like macleans.ca that: 1. Use non-standard content containers (not matching article-body, entry-content, etc.) 2. Have article content in nested div structures without standard class names 3. Include many navigation/sidebar links that overwhelm the content signal Improvements needed: - Add more content area selectors for common CMS patterns (WordPress, custom CMSes) - Add heuristic: if FindContentArea returns a container but ExtractParagraphs yields <3 paragraphs, try the next content selector instead of immediately falling through - Consider text density scoring: compare ratio of text length to HTML length in candidate content areas to find the actual article body - Add a fallback that looks for the largest contiguous block of

tags regardless of container class names - Log which content area selector matched (or didn't) for debugging
notes: REOPENED: Was closed without verification. Related to workspace-ddn4 (NYT article extraction). Acceptance: 1) Write test with JS-heavy page HTML fixture where extraction currently fails. 2) Fix extraction to handle the fixture. 3) Show test passing.
close_reason: Implemented: added 10+ CMS selectors (WordPress Gutenberg, Substack, Ghost, Drupal, data-attribute patterns), extended Level 2 to search <section> elements, added text density scoring (Level 2.5a) to find best container by text-to-HTML ratio when no named selector matches. 11 new tests covering all improvements. 2052 tests passing, 0 failures. Commit 9b9e5e0.

title: Live preload progress indicator in collection items view
description:
The status bar badge [caching X/Y] exists (StatusBarRenderer.cs:121) and GetProgress() works, but it only updates when the screen re-renders on user input. Since preloading happens in the background while the user is idle (not pressing keys), the progress counter appears frozen.

Fix: Add a lightweight background render tick that updates ONLY the status bar line (not the full screen) every ~1 second while preloading is active. This gives the user a clear, real-time indication that articles are being prefetched.

Options:
1. Timer-based: have the preload service emit a progress event after each fetch, and the orchestrator's render loop re-renders just the status bar line (using ANSI cursor positioning to overwrite line in-place)
2. Progress callback: PreloadService takes an IProgress<PreloadProgress> and the orchestrator subscribes to it for status bar updates
3. Collection item indicators: update per-item '· cached' suffix in real-time as each article is cached

The per-item indicator already exists in CollectionRenderer but only renders on redraw. The status bar badge is the easiest to update without disrupting the display.

Key files:
- src/TermReader.Infrastructure/Browser/UI/Renderers/StatusBarRenderer.cs
- src/TermReader.Infrastructure/Browser/Cache/BackgroundPreloadService.cs (progress events)
- src/TermReader.Infrastructure/Browser/BrowserOrchestrator.cs (render tick loop)
close_reason: Closed

title: Reading List should eagerly preload all items on entry
description:
The Reading List is a curated set of articles the user explicitly saved — it should be preloaded more aggressively than random links on a page. Currently it uses the same idle-gated, single-item-at-a-time preload as the hierarchical link view.

Changes:
1. When entering CollectionItems view for the Reading List, immediately trigger preloading (skip initial idle wait, or use a much shorter threshold like 500ms).
2. Consider preloading Reading List items on app startup in the background (after warmup), since the user is very likely to open it.
3. The Reading List preload should NOT enforce same-origin filtering (BuildCollectionQueue already skips this, which is correct).
4. Reduce the inter-fetch delay for Reading List items — use CrossDomainDelayMs (1500ms) as the default since reading list items are typically from diverse domains.

Key files:
- src/TermReader.Infrastructure/Browser/Cache/BackgroundPreloadService.cs (NotifyCollectionChanged, StartAsync)
- src/TermReader.Infrastructure/Browser/CommandHandlers/CollectionCommandHandler.cs (HandleOpenCollections)
- src/TermReader.Infrastructure/Browser/BrowserOrchestrator.cs (StartBrowsingAsync — startup preload)
close_reason: Closed

title: Foreground article load should get top priority over background preload
description:
When the user opens an article from a collection, the request should be the highest priority. Currently the Pause()/Resume() mechanism in BrowserOrchestrator.NavigateToAsync() pauses preloading during navigation, which prevents competition but doesn't prioritize the specific URL.

Needed changes:
1. When user activates a link, if that URL is already being prefetched in-flight, the foreground should piggyback on (or await) the in-flight task instead of starting a duplicate request. WaitForInFlightAsync exists but is only used in ReadingListContentProvider for podcasts.
2. Add a PrioritizeUrl(url) method to IPreloadService that moves a URL to the front of the queue — if the preload service has it queued but hasn't started fetching yet, the user gets it immediately from the preload result rather than making a separate request.
3. In BrowserOrchestrator.LoadPageAsync or NavigateToAsync: before doing a fresh fetch, check if preload has the result or is in-flight, and use that instead of a redundant fetch.

Key files:
- src/TermReader.Infrastructure/Browser/Cache/BackgroundPreloadService.cs (PrioritizeUrl, in-flight awareness)
- src/TermReader.Infrastructure/Browser/BrowserOrchestrator.cs (LoadPageAsync, NavigateToAsync)
- src/TermReader.Infrastructure/Browser/Cache/CachingPageLoader.cs (check in-flight before fetch)
close_reason: Closed

title: Force Selenium path for known-paywalled domains when cookies exist
description:
Even after wiring cookies, the HTTP-first strategy will still hit NYT's paywall because the HttpClient CookieContainer may not replicate the full browser session state NYT requires (JavaScript-set cookies, session tokens). For known-paywalled domains (nytimes.com, wsj.com, etc.), skip the HTTP attempt and go straight to Selenium when stored auth cookies exist.

Implementation:
1. Add a PaywalledDomains list to BrowserConfiguration (default: nytimes.com, wsj.com, washingtonpost.com)
2. In BrowserOrchestrator.LoadPageAsync: if URL domain matches PaywalledDomains AND cookies exist for that domain, skip HTTP and use Selenium directly
3. This avoids the latency of failing HTTP → falling back to Selenium, and ensures cookies are used from the start
4. If no cookies exist for the domain, fall back to normal HTTP-first behavior

Key files:
- src/TermReader.Infrastructure/Browser/BrowserOrchestrator.cs
- src/TermReader.Infrastructure/Configuration/BrowserConfiguration.cs
close_reason: Closed

title: Persist page cache to disk across app restarts
description: InMemoryPageCache is purely in-memory (ConcurrentDictionary) so all cached pages are lost when the app restarts. Articles that were already loaded during browsing must be re-fetched from scratch on the next launch, even if they were just viewed. The page cache should have a disk-backed persistence layer that survives app restarts. Requirements: (1) persist cached HTML to local storage (e.g. {LocalAppData}/TermReader/page-cache/), (2) TTL-based expiration so stale content is cleaned up, (3) total cache size capped at ~100MB with LRU eviction, (4) load persisted entries on startup so browsing and podcast generation benefit from previously cached pages. Note: ArticleContentCache already persists extracted article text for podcast generation, but the raw page cache used during browsing does not persist.
close_reason: Closed

title: GCP bucket setup must use service account key with explicit guided instructions
description:
The GCS integration should require a service account JSON key file for authentication — not Application Default Credentials (ADC). This prevents unauthorized uploads if someone discovers the bucket name.

## Service Account Setup Instructions (to be shown in-app)

The guided setup flow should walk the user through:

### 1. Create a Service Account
- Go to Google Cloud Console → IAM & Admin → Service Accounts
- Create a new service account (e.g., 'termreader-podcast')
- No special org-level roles needed

### 2. Grant Bucket Permissions
The service account needs these IAM roles on the target bucket (or project):
- **Storage Object Creator** (`roles/storage.objectCreator`) — upload files
- **Storage Object Viewer** (`roles/storage.objectViewer`) — read/check existing files (RSS feed)
- Or the combined **Storage Object Admin** (`roles/storage.objectAdmin`) for simplicity

If `CreateBucketIfNotExists` is true, also needs:
- **Storage Admin** (`roles/storage.admin`) on the project — to create buckets

### 3. Download the JSON Key
- On the service account page, go to Keys → Add Key → Create new key → JSON
- Save the downloaded .json file somewhere safe (e.g., ~/.config/termreader/gcs-key.json)
- This is the file path the app will ask for

### 4. What the App Needs
- **Service account key file path**: absolute path to the downloaded .json key file
- **Bucket name**: the GCS bucket name (3-63 chars, lowercase)
- The key file contains project ID, client email, and private key — no separate project ID input needed

## Implementation Changes

1. Remove ADC fallback from GcsStorageClient.GetClientAsync() — only allow GoogleCredential.FromFile()
2. Store the service account key path in IUserSettingsStore (key: 'GcsServiceAccountKeyPath', encrypted: true)
3. Update the setup flow to prompt for the key file path first, then bucket name
4. Validate key file exists and is valid JSON with required fields before proceeding
5. Show clear error if key file is missing/invalid rather than cryptic 'No GCP credentials found'
6. The bucket should NOT be public — access is controlled via the service account
notes: REOPENED 2026-03-14: Closed with no reason. This was the spec for GCP service account key setup. The guided flow described was never verified as working. Related to workspace-ww9m (guided GCP setup).
close_reason: Already implemented and tested in prior work - guided GCS setup with service account key validation, 3-step instructions, [k] handler, and bucket config all in place

title: Fix resource leaks in M4bAudioAssembler and OpenAiTtsService
description: HIGH: (1) M4bAudioAssembler.WriteChaptersAndMetadata (line 187): ATL.NET Track object not disposed — if Track.Save() fails, file handle leaks. Fix: use try-finally or using if Track implements IDisposable. (2) OpenAiTtsService (line 93): AudioClient created but never disposed — potential HTTP connection pool exhaustion. Fix: dispose AudioClient after generation loop. (3) M4bAudioAssembler.AssembleAsync (line 125): Directory.CreateDirectory not wrapped in try-catch — permission/disk errors give cryptic messages. (4) Cover art file read (line 231): TOCTOU race + no size validation. Fix: wrap in try-catch, add size limit.

title: Add cancellation token propagation fix in analysis finally block
description: PodcastCommandHandler.ShowCacheAnalysisScreenAsync finally block (line 355-368) awaits the analysis task after cancellation without a timeout. If the analysis task ignores the cancellation token (e.g. stuck in a synchronous Selenium call), the finally block hangs indefinitely. Add a timeout (e.g. 5s) to the finally await, and if it times out, detach the task.

title: Add graceful degradation when Selenium driver crashes mid-extraction
description: During multi-article extraction, if Chrome crashes on article N (WebDriverException), the BrowserSession driver reference becomes stale. Subsequent articles that fall through to Selenium Layer 2/3 will also fail with driver errors, wasting time on retries that can never succeed. ReadingListContentProvider should track whether the Selenium driver has crashed and skip Selenium layers for remaining articles, falling back to HTTP-only extraction.
close_reason: Closed

title: Add top-level exception guard to PodcastOrchestrator.AnalyzeCacheStatusAsync
description: If GetAllArticleContentAsync or AnalyzeCollectionAsync throws an unexpected exception (not OperationCanceledException), the podcast flow crashes. While ShowCacheAnalysisScreenAsync has a try-catch around the analysisTask await, AnalyzeCacheStatusAsync itself should wrap its body in a try-catch that logs the error and returns an empty CacheAnalysis instead of propagating. Defense-in-depth.
close_reason: Closed

title: Fix Process handle leak in M4bAudioAssembler.ValidatePrerequisitesAsync
description: M4bAudioAssembler.ValidatePrerequisitesAsync (line 32-59) creates a System.Diagnostics.Process to run 'ffmpeg -version' but never disposes it. The Process object leaks OS handles. Wrap the process in a using statement.
close_reason: Closed

title: WaitForInputAsync drops key presses after terminal resize events
description: In TerminalInputHandler.WaitForInputAsync (line 32-92), when a resize event wins the Task.WhenAny race against a key channel ReadAsync, the pending key ReadAsync is abandoned but stays in the channel reader queue. This abandoned reader consumes the next key press, silently dropping it. Each resize event causes one subsequent key press to be lost. The fix should either: (a) use a CancellationTokenSource to cancel the losing task when the other wins, (b) redesign to avoid concurrent readers on the same channel (e.g., use a single combined event queue), or (c) track the pending key task and reuse it like ShowCacheWaitScreenAsync does with pendingKeyTask ??= pattern.
close_reason: Promoted pending key/resize ReadAsync tasks to instance fields with ??= reuse. Added DrainPendingTasks for PromptForInputAsync safety. Added faulted task cleanup for crash recovery.

title: Fix misleading 'after N retries' error for non-retried auth failures
description:
In OpenAiTtsService.GenerateChunkWithRetryAsync, when a 401 or 403 error occurs, the code returns null immediately (correct — no retry). But the caller in GenerateAudioAsync line 113 reports 'Failed to generate audio for chunk X/Y after N retries' which is misleading since auth errors are never retried. Fix: make GenerateChunkWithRetryAsync return a richer result that includes the failure reason (auth error vs. exhausted retries vs. unexpected error). Then update the error message in GenerateAudioAsync to say:
  - 401/403: 'Authentication failed for chunk X/Y — check your API key'
  - 429/5xx exhausted: 'Failed to generate audio for chunk X/Y after N retries (rate limited/server error)'
  - Other: 'Unexpected error generating chunk X/Y: {message}'
This gives users actionable information.
close_reason: Replaced misleading 'after N retries' with specific messages: auth failed (401/403), bad request (400), retries exhausted (429/5xx), unexpected error (catch-all). Uses ChunkGenerationResult record struct. Committed as e82ba04.

title: ReadingListContentProvider: wait for in-flight preloads before fetching

If so, wait for it to complete (with a short timeout, e.g. 15s) and then check the cache. This avoids duplicate HTTP fetches and ensures we benefit from preloading that's almost done. Implementation: add an optional IPreloadService dependency (or expose an 'await in-flight' method on IPageCache). If the URL becomes cached while waiting, skip the HTTP fetch and use the cached result.

title: Start headed Chrome minimized to avoid covering terminal
description: BrowserSession.CreateChromeDriver (line 208) sets --window-size=1400,900 for headed mode, which covers the terminal. Fix: add --start-minimized to ChromeOptions when creating in headed mode. The browser stays out of the way for background Selenium automation. When interactive browsing is needed (e.g., HandleInteractiveBrowserPause for bot challenges), restore the window via driver.Manage().Window.Maximize() or .Normal. Also consider using driver.Manage().Window.Minimize() after the anti-detection JS injection (line 234) as a fallback. Files: BrowserSession.cs (CreateChromeDriver, lines 188-240), BrowserOrchestrator.cs (HandleInteractiveBrowserPause).

title: Skip eager browser warmup in headed mode
description: Program.cs:98-111 calls session.WarmUpAsync() in a background task at startup regardless of headless setting. When Browser:Headless is false (appsettings.json:31), this immediately opens a visible Chrome window showing 'data:,' before the user has navigated anywhere. Fix: gate the warmup on headless mode — only call WarmUpAsync() when _browserConfig.Headless is true. In headed mode, let the browser be created lazily on first actual navigation (the 2-3s cold-start penalty is acceptable since the user is already waiting for a page load). Files: Program.cs (warmup call), BrowserSession.cs (WarmUpAsync), BrowserConfiguration.cs (Headless property).
close_reason: Both implemented: skip warmup in headed mode, start Chrome minimized/offscreen, restore on interactive refresh. 1447 tests pass.

title: Fix chapter marker duration estimation using actual audio duration
description: PodcastOrchestrator line 199-201 estimates duration from character count (chars / (150*5.0) minutes). This estimate is passed to ArticleAudioSegment and used for chapter markers. M4bAudioAssembler uses estimated duration when segment.Duration != TimeSpan.Zero, so it uses the WRONG estimated value instead of probing the real audio duration via FFProbe. Fix: either set Duration to TimeSpan.Zero so the assembler probes, or use FFProbe in the orchestrator to get the real duration after TTS generation.

title: Podcast Orchestrator (End-to-End Pipeline)
description:
Create the orchestrator that coordinates all podcast pipeline components into a single end-to-end flow.

FILE: src/TermReader.Infrastructure/Podcast/PodcastOrchestrator.cs

INTERFACE: src/TermReader.Application/Interfaces/Podcast/IPodcastOrchestrator.cs
- Task<PodcastResult> GeneratePodcastAsync(Collection collection, IProgress<PodcastProgress>? progress, ct)

DEPENDENCIES:
- ReadingListContentProvider (cache pre-warming + content extraction)
- ITtsService (OpenAI TTS)
- IAudioAssembler (M4B assembly)
- IPodcastPublisher (GCS upload + feed)
- IOptions<PodcastConfiguration>
- IOptions<OpenAiTtsConfiguration>
- ILogger

PIPELINE STEPS:
1. Pre-flight: Check ITtsService.IsConfigured, IAudioAssembler.ValidatePrerequisitesAsync
2. Pre-warm cache: ReadingListContentProvider.GetAllArticleContentAsync (reports progress)
3. Estimate cost: Sum ITtsService.EstimateCost for each article, check total against MaxBudgetUsd
4. Generate TTS: For each article in order, call ITtsService.GenerateAudioAsync, save AAC chunks to temp files (reports per-article progress)
5. Assemble M4B: Call IAudioAssembler.AssembleAsync with all article segments + metadata
6. Publish: Call IPodcastPublisher.PublishFeedAsync to upload M4B + generate/upload RSS feed
7. Return PodcastResult with feed URL, file path, stats

PROGRESS REPORTING:
- PodcastProgress DTO: Phase (CachingContent, GeneratingAudio, AssemblingAudio, Publishing), CurrentArticle, TotalArticles, ArticleTitle, PercentComplete
- Must be granular enough for the UI to show per-article status

ERROR HANDLING:
- Pre-flight failures → return immediately with error
- Individual article TTS failure → skip, log, continue
- Assembly failure → return error with partial info
- Publish failure → return error but note local M4B file path

RESULT DTO:
- PodcastResult: Success, FeedUrl?, LocalFilePath?, TotalDuration, ArticlesProcessed, ArticlesFailed, FileSizeBytes, ErrorMessage?
close_reason: PodcastOrchestrator implemented with full pipeline, DTOs, interface, DI registration, and tests. Agent-reviewed and all issues fixed.

title: Cache Pre-warming for Reading List
description:
Ensure all reading list items have their content cached before podcast generation begins.

PROBLEM: The page cache (IPageCache/InMemoryPageCache) is in-memory only and stores raw HTML. When an article is added to the reading list, it may or may not be in the cache (depends on whether user visited it recently). For podcast generation, we need the ReadableContent (extracted text) for every item.

SOLUTION: Before TTS generation, iterate through all reading list items and:
1. Check if URL is in IPageCache (cache.Contains(url))
2. If cached: extract ReadableContent from cached HTML via IReadableContentExtractor
3. If NOT cached: load the page via IPageLoader (which will auto-cache via CachingPageLoader decorator)
4. Then extract ReadableContent from the loaded HTML

IMPLEMENTATION: Add a method to the PodcastOrchestrator or create a ReadingListContentProvider:
- File: src/TermReader.Infrastructure/Podcast/ReadingListContentProvider.cs
- Method: Task<IReadOnlyList<ArticleContent>> GetAllArticleContentAsync(Collection collection, IProgress<T>? progress, ct)
- Returns ordered list of (Title, CleanedText, Author, Url) for each collection item
- Reports progress as each article is fetched/extracted
- Uses existing IPageCache.TryGet() and IReadableContentExtractor.ExtractAsync()
- Falls back to IPageLoader.LoadAsync() for uncached items
- IMPORTANT: Must respect rate limiting when fetching uncached pages (use existing delay patterns)
- Skip items that fail extraction (log warning, continue)

INTEGRATION POINT: This is called before the TTS step in the podcast pipeline.
close_reason: ReadingListContentProvider with cache-aware loading, rate limiting, and progress reporting

title: GCS Storage Client & Podcast Publisher
description:
Implement Google Cloud Storage client and the podcast publishing orchestrator.

GCS STORAGE CLIENT (src/TermReader.Infrastructure/Podcast/GcsStorageClient.cs):
- Implements ICloudStorageClient
- Constructor: IOptions<GcsConfiguration>, ILogger
- Auth: ServiceAccountKeyPath → GoogleCredential.FromFile(), else Application Default Credentials
- Bucket setup on first use: create if CreateBucketIfNotExists=true, UniformBucketLevelAccess=true, IAM roles/storage.objectViewer for allUsers (public read, no listing)
- UploadAsync: stream content with ContentType and CacheControl headers
- URL format: https://storage.googleapis.com/{bucket}/{objectPath}
- DownloadStringAsync, ExistsAsync for feed management

PODCAST PUBLISHER (src/TermReader.Infrastructure/Podcast/PodcastPublisher.cs):
- Implements IPodcastPublisher
- Constructor: ICloudStorageClient, IPodcastFeedGenerator, ILogger
- GCS path structure: podcasts/{feed-uuid}/feed.xml, podcasts/{feed-uuid}/episodes/{ep-uuid}.m4b, podcasts/{feed-uuid}/manifest.json
- Feed index at podcasts/feed-index.json (maps podcast title → feed UUID)

PublishFeedAsync flow:
1. GetExistingFeedUrlAsync to find/reuse feed UUID
2. Generate new UUID if first publish (128 bits entropy, unguessable)
3. Upload each episode M4B (skip if already uploaded via ExistsAsync)
4. Build EpisodeMetadata with GCS audio URLs
5. Call IPodcastFeedGenerator to generate feed XML
6. Upload feed.xml (replace existing)
7. Update manifest.json and feed-index.json
8. Return FeedPublishResult with stable feed URL

SECURITY:
- Bucket listing disabled (no storage.buckets.list for allUsers)
- UUID paths provide 122 bits of entropy (computationally infeasible to guess)
- podcast:locked=yes prevents directory crawling

NuGet: Google.Cloud.Storage.V1
close_reason: GCS storage client and podcast publisher implemented with deterministic episode IDs and episode merging

title: RSS Podcast Feed Generator
description:
Implement RSS 2.0 podcast feed generation with iTunes and Podcasting 2.0 extensions.

FILE: src/TermReader.Infrastructure/Podcast/PodcastFeedGenerator.cs

- Uses System.Xml.Linq (XDocument/XElement/XNamespace) - already available, follows OpmlExporter pattern

RSS NAMESPACES:
- xmlns:itunes='http://www.itunes.com/dtds/podcast-1.0.dtd'
- xmlns:podcast='https://podcastindex.org/namespace/1.0'
- xmlns:psc='http://podlove.org/simple-chapters'

CHANNEL-LEVEL TAGS:
- Standard RSS 2.0: title, link, description, language, lastBuildDate, generator
- iTunes: itunes:author, itunes:summary, itunes:image, itunes:category, itunes:explicit, itunes:type='episodic'
- Podcasting 2.0: podcast:locked='yes' (prevent directory crawling)

ITEM-LEVEL TAGS:
- Standard: title, description, pubDate (RFC 2822), guid (isPermaLink=false, episode UUID)
- Enclosure: url (GCS audio URL), length (bytes), type='audio/x-m4b'
- iTunes: itunes:duration (HH:MM:SS format), itunes:summary
- Podlove Simple Chapters: psc:chapters with psc:chapter elements (start='HH:MM:SS.mmm', title, href)
- Podcasting 2.0: podcast:chapters (optional, URL to JSON chapters file)

APPLE PODCASTS REQUIREMENTS:
- itunes:image must be referenced (square, 1400x1400 to 3000x3000)
- itunes:category from Apple's predefined list
- Valid enclosure with length and type
- podcast:locked=yes

Method: Task<string> GenerateFeedXmlAsync(PodcastMetadata, IReadOnlyList<EpisodeMetadata>, ct)
Returns XML as string. Pure function from metadata to XML - no file I/O, no cloud deps.
close_reason: Implemented PodcastFeedGenerator with RSS 2.0, iTunes/Podcasting 2.0/Podlove namespaces, CDATA descriptions, chapter markers

title: M4B Audio Assembler
description:
Implement the audio assembly pipeline that concatenates article audio segments into a single M4B file with Nero chapter markers.

TEMP FILE MANAGER (src/TermReader.Infrastructure/Podcast/TempFileManager.cs):
- IDisposable utility for managing temp directory lifecycle
- Creates unique temp dir, tracks files, cleanup on dispose
- CreateConcatListFile() generates ffmpeg concat demuxer format

M4B AUDIO ASSEMBLER (src/TermReader.Infrastructure/Podcast/M4bAudioAssembler.cs):
- Implements IAudioAssembler
- Constructor: ILogger, IOptions<PodcastConfiguration>
- ValidatePrerequisitesAsync: checks FFmpeg is installed via FFMpegHelper
- AssembleAsync pipeline:
  1. Validate inputs (segments non-empty, chunk files exist)
  2. Phase A: Concatenate intra-article chunks (codec copy via concat demuxer)
  3. Phase B: FFProbe duration probing → compute chapter timestamps
  4. Phase C: Concatenate all articles → single M4B (re-encode: aac, 64k, mono, 44.1kHz, -movflags +faststart)
  5. Phase D: Write Nero chapter markers via ATL.NET (ATL.Settings.MP4_createNeroChapters=true, ChapterInfo with StartTime in ms)
  6. Phase E: Write file metadata via ATL.NET (Title, Artist, Genre, Description, Date, optional cover art)
  7. Cleanup temp files
  8. Return AssemblyResult with chapter list and file info

FFMpegCore usage:
- FFMpegArguments.FromDemuxConcatInput(paths).OutputToFile(path, overwrite, opts => opts.CopyChannel()) for chunk concat
- FFMpegArguments.FromDemuxConcatInput(paths).OutputToFile(path, overwrite, opts => opts.WithAudioCodec('aac').WithAudioBitrate(64)...) for final assembly
- FFProbe.AnalyseAsync() for duration probing

NuGet deps: FFMpegCore, z440.atl.core (both added in NuGet task)
close_reason: Implemented M4bAudioAssembler (FFMpegCore concat + ATL.NET chapters/metadata in single save) and TempFileManager

title: OpenAI TTS Service + TextChunker
description:
Implement the OpenAI text-to-speech service and text chunking utility.

TEXT CHUNKER (src/TermReader.Infrastructure/Podcast/TextChunker.cs):
- Static utility: IReadOnlyList<string> ChunkText(string text, int maxChunkSize=4096)
- Algorithm: split at paragraph boundaries (\n\n), then sentence boundaries, then word boundaries, then hard-split
- Every chunk <= maxChunkSize, no empty chunks, no content loss
- Independently testable pure function

OPENAI TTS SERVICE (src/TermReader.Infrastructure/Podcast/OpenAiTtsService.cs):
- Implements ITtsService
- Constructor: IOptions<OpenAiTtsConfiguration>, ILogger<OpenAiTtsService>
- IsConfigured: checks config ApiKey, then OPENAI_API_KEY env var
- EstimateCost(text): character count, chunk count, USD at $15/1M chars, estimated duration (~150 wpm)
- GenerateAudioAsync: pre-flight (IsConfigured, budget check), chunk text, process sequentially with IProgress<TtsProgress> reporting, inter-chunk delay for rate limiting, concatenate AAC bytes
- SetApiKeyOverride(string): allows runtime key injection from UI prompt
- Uses OpenAI NuGet AudioClient with GenerateSpeech()
- Manual exponential backoff retry (not Polly): retry on 429/5xx, no retry on 401/400
- Maps voice string to GeneratedSpeechVoice enum, format string to GeneratedSpeechFormat

Key patterns to follow:
- IOptions<T> for config (like PageLoader)
- Static factory result methods (like PageLoadResult.Successful/Failure)
- Serilog logging conventions

title: Add debounce to NotifySelectionChanged
description: When user holds j/k to scroll quickly, NotifySelectionChanged fires on every movement, rebuilding the queue each time. This prevents the preload service from completing any pre-fetch. Add a 200ms debounce: store the latest selection change, only rebuild the queue if 200ms passes with no further changes. This is especially important with top-to-bottom ordering, which resets to index 0 on every rebuild. File: src/TermReader.Infrastructure/Browser/Cache/BackgroundPreloadService.cs NotifySelectionChanged method.

title: Retry with headed browser when headless Selenium gets challenge page
description: The content-quality fallback in LoadPageAsync only retries when FetchMethod != Selenium. But when headless Selenium gets a DataDome challenge, it IS Selenium so no retry happens. Need a second fallback: if Selenium load results in a detected bot challenge page AND config allows headed mode, retry with Headless=false to give DataDome a better chance of passing (headed Chrome is harder to fingerprint). This requires BrowserSession to support switching from headless to headed (may need to dispose current driver and create new one). Files: BrowserOrchestrator.cs (LoadPageAsync), BrowserSession.cs (driver recreation), PageLoader.cs (challenge detection result).
